class Solution {
    public String arrangeWords(String text) {
        String[] words = text.split(" ");
        List<Integer> indices = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i=0; i < words.length; i++) {
            indices.add(i);
        }
        // sort the indices based on word length and original index
        Collections.sort(indices, (a, b) -> {
            int lenA = words[a].length(), lenB = words[b].length();
            if (lenA != lenB) {
                return lenA - lenB;
            } else {
                return a - b;
            }
        });
        // create the sorted words in lowercase
        List<String> sortedWords = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i : indices) {
            sortedWords.add( words[i].toLowerCase() );
        }
        // capitalize first letter
        if (!sortedWords.isEmpty()) {
            String first = sortedWords.get(0);
            sortedWords.set(0, Character.toUpperCase(first.charAt(0)) + first.substring(1));
        }
        return String.join(" ", sortedWords);
    }
}

Yes.

Now, what about the time and space complexity?

Time complexity: splitting is O(n), where n is the length of the text. Then, creating the indices list is O(m) where m is the number of words. Sorting the indices is O(m log m) time. Then processing the sorted words is O(m). So overall O(n + m log m). Since m can be up to (n+1)/1 (if each character is a word, but in practice, since each word has at least one character, m is O(n)). So overall time is O(n + m log m) → O(n log n) in worst case.

Space: the indices list is O(m), and the sortedWords list is O(m). So O(m) space. Which is acceptable.

Now, for Java, the code seems correct. Let's test for edge cases. For example, a single word input. Input: "Hello" → output "Hello". The code splits into ["Hello"], sorted indices [0], converted to lowercase "hello", then first letter capitalized → "Hello".

Another case: two words with same length. Input: "Ab cD" → split into ["Ab", "cD"]. After processing, sorted by original order. So sorted indices [0,1]. Lowercase → "ab", "cd". Capitalize first → "Ab cd".

So output is "Ab cd".

solution: 
```class Solution {
    public String arrangeWords(String text) {
        String[] words = text.split(" ");
        List<Integer> indices = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < words.length; i++) {
            indices.add(i);
        }
        Collections.sort(indices, (a, b) -> {
            int lenA = words[a].length(), lenB = words[b].length();
            if (lenA != lenB) {
                return lenA - lenB;
            } else {
                return a - b;
            }
        });
        List<String> sortedWords = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i : indices) {
            sortedWords.add(words[i].toLowerCase());
        }
        if (!sortedWords.isEmpty()) {
            String first = sortedWords.get(0);
            sortedWords.set(0, Character.toUpperCase(first.charAt(0)) + first.substring(1));
        }
        return String.join(" ", sortedWords);
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N + M log M)
space complexity: O(M)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution splits the text into words, sorts their indices based on word lengths and original positions, converts words to lowercase, capitalizes the first word, and joins them. It efficiently handles the stable sort requirement using a custom comparator and maintains original order for same-length words.
